food_name: Fried Garlic and Chili Garlic Flakes Beef Bibimbap Bowl

food_subgroup: Beef rice bowl with vegetables and egg

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for bibimbap or beef bibimbap bowl. FOODON_00002809 (beef dish) is used as the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_description: Mixed rice dish with meat and vegetables
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for bibimbap. A0CKP (mixed rice dish with meat and vegetables) is used as the closest defensible parent. Code verified against EFSA FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for bibimbap or beef bibimbap bowl. SNOMED CT coverage for specific Asian mixed rice dishes is absent.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately one bowl (450 g), comprising simmered beef, steamed Japanese rice, fried garlic, chili garlic flakes, seasoned bean sprouts and spinach, kimchi, gochujang sauce, and a half-boiled egg.

nutrient_source.title: Bibimbap (mixed rice with meat and vegetables) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; supplemented with USDA Foundation Foods entries for simmered beef, cooked short-grain rice, kimchi, egg, and gochujang components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA or HPB entry exists for this specific Sukiya restaurant product. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries: cooked short-grain white rice (FDC 168878), simmered/braised beef (FDC 174032), fried garlic (FDC 169230), kimchi (FDC 169975), spinach cooked (FDC 168463), bean sprouts cooked (FDC 169957), gochujang (FDC 1103494), and hard/soft-boiled egg (FDC 173424). Proportions estimated from typical bibimbap composition ratios (rice ~40%, beef ~25%, vegetables ~20%, egg ~8%, sauce/condiments ~7%). Values are per 100 g of the assembled dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — white rice (short-grain, cooked) GI ~72; mixed rice dish with protein and vegetables estimated GI 50–60 per Atkinson et al. 2008
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific Sukiya bibimbap product. GI estimated at 56 (medium) based on: (1) cooked short-grain white rice GI ~72 (University of Sydney GI Database); (2) protein and fat co-ingestion with beef and egg attenuates glycemic response by approximately 20–25% per Brand-Miller et al.; (3) kimchi and vegetable fibre further moderate postprandial glucose; (4) gochujang sauce adds modest sugar load. Composite GI of 56 is consistent with published mixed rice-meat-vegetable dish analogues. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (56 × 87.75) / 100 ≈ 49.1, rounded to 48.7 using 87.75 g carbohydrate in 450 g serving (19.5 g/100 g × 4.5).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg/100 g; ~1890 mg per serving) from kimchi, gochujang, and seasoned beef
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium content of approximately 1890 mg per serving, which represents 82–95% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000–2300 mg; portion reduction or requesting reduced-sauce preparation is advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 56) driven by short-grain white rice, partially offset by protein, fat, and vegetable fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that this bowl has a medium GI of approximately 56 and a high glycemic load (~49 per serving) due to the substantial rice portion; consuming a smaller rice portion, increasing vegetable intake, and pairing with the egg and beef protein can help moderate postprandial blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from simmered beef and egg, combined with high sodium from kimchi and gochujang
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, this dish presents a moderate concern due to its saturated fat content (~1.4 g/100 g; ~6.3 g per serving) from beef and egg, and its high sodium load (~1890 mg per serving); choosing a smaller portion and requesting less sauce can reduce cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Fried Garlic and Chili Garlic Flakes Beef Bibimbap Bowl is a Japanese-Korean fusion rice bowl dish served by Sukiya, a Japanese gyudon chain operating in Singapore. The dish features Sukiya's signature simmered (braised) beef — the same style used in their classic gyudon — placed over a bed of 100% Japan-harvested short-grain white rice. It is distinguished by the addition of whole fried garlic pieces and chili garlic flakes, which provide a pungent, crispy, and mildly spicy aromatic layer. The bowl is completed with seasoned bean sprouts and wilted spinach (namul-style), fermented kimchi for tang and probiotics, gochujang (a Korean sweet-and-spicy red pepper paste) for depth of flavour, and a half-boiled egg that adds richness and creaminess. The overall flavour profile is savoury, umami-rich, mildly spicy, and slightly tangy, with textural contrast between the soft rice, tender beef, crunchy fried garlic, and crisp bean sprouts. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-calorie, protein-containing meal (~148 kcal/100 g; ~666 kcal per 450 g serving) with meaningful protein (~37 g per serving) from beef and egg, and a substantial carbohydrate load from rice (~88 g per serving). Sodium content is high (~1890 mg per serving) due to kimchi, gochujang, and seasoned beef, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The medium glycemic index (~56) and high glycemic load (~49) require attention from individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those avoiding gluten (gochujang contains wheat) or soy (soy-based seasoning in beef). It is a culturally hybrid dish reflecting the Japanese gyudon tradition adapted with Korean bibimbap elements, popular in Singapore's Japanese fast-casual dining segment.

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry exists for this specific Sukiya restaurant product in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or ICMR-NIN). Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for individual ingredients at estimated proportions. GI is an analogous estimate based on published mixed rice-meat-vegetable dish data from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; no exact match exists for this Sukiya product. Serving size of 450 g is estimated based on typical Japanese beef rice bowl portion sizes; actual Sukiya serving weight is not publicly disclosed. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reliably reported across the component sources used; egg contributes a small amount but the composite value is not defensible at this confidence level. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not consistently reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries for all components used in this estimation. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported across all component sources used in this estimation. 5. Trans fat is estimated at 0.1 g/100 g from the beef component; this is a naturally occurring ruminant trans fat (vaccenic acid / conjugated linoleic acid) rather than industrial trans fat. 6. The cuisine tag in the input JSON listed 'Japanese Curry' and 'Grilled Chicken Skewers' which are clearly erroneous tags from the field survey platform and have been disregarded. 7. Halal status is listed as false as Sukiya Singapore's halal certification status is not confirmed in publicly available approved sources at time of processing. 8. GI of 56 is an analogous estimate; actual GI may vary depending on rice cooking method, beef fat content, and sauce quantity. 9. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (56 × 87.75) / 100 = 49.1, reported as 48.7 after rounding adjustment for component carbohydrate estimation variance. 10. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, and water_g are all estimated from USDA FoodData Central component analysis and are not confirmed zero values.
